roots.title: Introduction to Simple Linear Regression and MSE Derivation
roots.summary: The segment introduces simple linear regression (SLR) modeling, emphasizing its importance in data analysis and providing context for the course Data 100/Data 200 at UC Berkeley. It discusses the modeling phase, highlighting mathematical derivations and evaluations, starting with SLR and advancing to multiple regression. The significance of models as idealized representations is underscored, including the importance of assumptions and a quote from George Box about the utility of flawed models. The discussion contrasts deterministic and probabilistic models, using examples like brain wave analysis. Additionally, the segment covers the calculation of Mean Squared Error (MSE) derivatives with respect to model parameters, explaining the process of optimization through partial derivatives and the derivation of Theta 1 hat in regression.

children.title: Introduction to Simple Linear Regression Modeling
children.summary: The segment introduces the concept of modeling in data analysis, specifically focusing on the simple linear regression (SLR) model. It emphasizes the significance of models for understanding data and provides context for the course, Data 100/Data 200, Fall 2024 at UC Berkeley, taught by Narges Norouzi and Joseph E. Gonzalez.

children.title: Introduction to Simple Linear Regression Modeling
children.summary: The segment introduces modeling in data analysis with a focus on the simple linear regression (SLR) model. It highlights the importance of models for understanding data and provides context for the course, Data 100/Data 200, Fall 2024 at UC Berkeley, taught by Narges Norouzi and Joseph E. Gonzalez.

children.title: Introduction to Modeling and SLR
children.summary: The segment introduces the topic of modeling in data analysis, specifically focusing on the simple linear regression (SLR) model. It emphasizes the usefulness of models in understanding data and sets the context for the course, Data 100/Data 200, Fall 2024 at UC Berkeley, led by Narges Norouzi and Joseph E. Gonzalez.

children.title: Introduction to Modeling and Regression Concepts
children.summary: The segment introduces the modeling phase of Data 100, focusing on mathematical derivations and model evaluations, starting with simple linear regression and advancing to multiple regression. It explains that models in data science are idealized representations that simplify complexities to simulate trends, emphasizing the significance of assumptions in modeling, such as in population data. A quote from George Box highlights that while all models are flawed, they can still be useful for explaining phenomena, making predictions, and establishing variable relationships, with examples like spam classification and height correlation. The discussion contrasts deterministic models, governed by physical laws, with probabilistic models that handle randomness, using brain wave analysis as an example for predicting anxiety and seizure occurrences.

children.title: Introduction to Modeling and Simple Linear Regression
children.summary: The upcoming lectures will focus on the modeling phase of Data 100, emphasizing mathematical derivations and model evaluations. An initial review will cover simple linear regression and the concept of modeling, progressing towards more advanced models, including multiple regression, which involves predicting one variable based on multiple attributes.

children.title: Introduction to Modeling and Simple Linear Regression
children.summary: The upcoming lectures will focus on the modeling phase of Data 100, emphasizing mathematical derivations and model evaluations. The initial review will cover simple linear regression and the concept of modeling, progressing towards more advanced models, including multiple regression, which involves predicting one variable based on multiple attributes.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So kind of telling you, we're at today and we're going in the next couple of lectures is that we're starting actually digging deeper into the modeling phase of data 100. So it's going to be really exciting. I'm going to try to do a lot of math derivation and also building different models with you and also evaluating different, the performance of different models with you throughout today's lecture and also in the next two lectures to come. So this is where we are. We're going to start by reviewing what you've seen in in data 8, we're going to start by taking a look at what was the simple linear regression and what is modeling and what not. And we're going to go a little bit deeper into more advanced models up to the point that in the last lecture of the regression sequence, we're going to talk about multiple regression. So what happens when you have multiple different attributes that you want to model and you want to combine them into predicting one variable? Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

children.title: Understanding Models and Predictive Modeling in Data Science
children.summary: Models in data science are idealized representations of systems that simulate trends while simplifying complexities. For example, modeling an object's fall under gravity demonstrates the need for assumptions, as real-world factors like air resistance can affect accuracy. In Data 100, the importance of simplifying assumptions when modeling population data is emphasized, acknowledging that noise can cause data points to deviate from trends. A quote from George Box states that all models are wrong but some are useful. Models serve three primary purposes: explaining complex phenomena, making accurate predictions about unseen data, and creating interpretable relationships between variables. Examples include spam email classification and the correlation between parents' and children's heights. The final project involves classifying emails as spam or not spam.

children.title: Understanding Models in Data Science
children.summary: A model is defined as an idealized representation of a system, used to simulate or summarize trends accurately, despite not capturing all details or noise. For instance, modeling the fall of an object under constant gravity illustrates how simplifications are made; while gravity is typically approximated as 9.81 m/s², factors like elevation and air resistance affect its accuracy. This highlights the necessity of simplifying assumptions to create usable models that reflect the underlying dynamics of the system.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is a roadmap for today. We're going to start by talking about what is a model. Most of you might know what is a model or like you've used the term that there is a machine learning model and whatnot before, but just to tell you what a model is from our perspective in data 100 is that we always think about a model as an idealized representation of a system. So we say that it's an idealized representation of the system because it's not always accurate, we're not always considering all the details and all different features and all the noise that's involved in the system, but we're trying to actually have a model that kind of simulates or summarizes the trend that's happening in a system as accurately as possible. So an example of that could be modeling the fall of an object, an Earth subject to constant gravity that we always think about that as the gravity as being 9.81 meter per second square, but we all know that depending on the elevation, depending on the air resistance, this number is not accurate. So it's not going to be a constant model or a constant valley that we can use in order to model the gravity. So this is an example that we simplify assumptions, so that we have a model that's kind of roughly accurate. It's usable. It's simplifying all the dynamics of the, the dynamics in the underlying nature of the system that we have. Video: 1: V

children.title: Modeling Population Data and Complex Relationships
children.summary: The focus is on modeling population data in Data 100, emphasizing the importance of simplifying assumptions when sampling. It discusses how not all data points will align with the modeled trend, introducing the idea of noise in data. A quote from George Box highlights that while all models are wrong, some can be useful. Two main reasons for modeling are presented: first, to understand complex relationships, such as the correlation between parents' and children's heights, and second, to achieve accurate predictions by potentially using numerous parameters in deep learning architectures.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And that's actually what we're going to do in data 100 as well. Because what you've seen in the previous lecture is that we want to model for population, but we don't have the population. We're trying to simplify assumptions, say that okay, we're sampling. Our dataset is a good sample and is a good representation of the population. And now we're going to do the modeling. And not all of our data points are going to follow the trend that we're going to to model. So those are kind of the noises that we're trying to also avoid or not directly model. So there's a famous saying by George Box that says essentially all mothers are wrung but some are useful, which is exactly what you should expect from all the modeling work that we'll be doing in data 100 as well. we want to build models. And two of these reasons you might see them in data 100 this semester. Reason one is that there's a complex phenomenon that we want to actually model. So for example, we want to figure out our parents average heights related to chillers average heights. So there's like all of those complex relationships that we can study directly, but we can actually collect a bunch of data and then model based on or create a model based on those assumptions or those data points that we have. So in this case, what we would say is that the concept that we're modeling is very complex. What we want to have is a very simple model that is also interpretable, but it's also at the same time reasonably accurate. We don't want to try and random kind of estimations of the values that we want. The second reason is that We might want to have really accurate productions we have a good amount of data we want to make sure that we have very good accurate productions and in doing so what we will do is we'll throw in multiple different parameters not multiple but millions of parameters into a deep learning architecture for example in order to have a very accurate production. So what's happening when we want to use these black box models, we call them black box models because there's millions of different parameters. It's not easily, we cannot easily identify what's the relationship between them or what is the interaction between different parameters that is bits out, the actual estimation that we have at the end. But what we know is that over the population or over the sample that we have, we would say that it's accurate enough that we can actually use it. And it's more accurate than most of the interpretable models or simple models that we've seen in the first case. Video: 1: X

children.title: Reasons for Building Predictive Models
children.summary: Models are built for three main reasons: to explain complex phenomena occurring in the world, to make accurate predictions about unseen data, and to create interpretable models that clarify relationships between variables. Examples include predicting if an email is spam, understanding the relationship between parents' and children's heights, and summarizing lengthy articles. While some models prioritize accuracy and may become black-box models, others focus on simplicity and interpretability.

children.title: Project Overview: Email Classification in Data 100
children.summary: Students will classify emails as spam or not spam in the final project for Data 100, using provided emails with subject lines and body text. The segment also discusses three reasons for building models: to explain complex phenomena, to make accurate predictions about unseen data, and to generate concise summaries of lengthy articles. It highlights the trade-off between model interpretability and accuracy, noting that simpler, interpretable models are preferred in some contexts, while black-box models are often used in deep learning for their predictive power.

children.title: Reasons for Building Models in Data Science
children.summary: Models are built for three main reasons: to explain complex phenomena, to make accurate predictions about unseen data, and to understand relationships between variables. For example, predicting if an email is spam relates to making accurate predictions, while understanding the relationship between parents' and children's heights involves explaining complex phenomena. Additionally, there is a trade-off between model interpretability and accuracy; simpler, interpretable models help understand variable relationships, while black-box models, common in deep learning, prioritize accuracy over interpretability.

children.title: Causal Inference and Model Interpretability
children.summary: The importance of achieving accurate predictions in modeling is emphasized, even if it compromises interpretability. Causal inference, which is not covered in Data 100 but relevant for advanced courses, is introduced as a method to determine if one variable causes another, using smoking and lung cancer as an example. The discussion highlights the need to balance interpretability and accuracy in models, advocating for models that are both interpretable and sufficiently accurate. Common types of models mentioned include mechanistic or deterministic models, which adhere to a non-probabilistic approach based on empirical measurements.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is very important. In these cases, we would say that the reason that we want to do the model is to have a very accurate prediction, even if it comes at the cost of the model, not being interpretable. Reason tree is a reason that we will not go over in data 100, but that's a topic for more advanced courses, some stats courses or data 102 covers causal inference. So for example, if you want to figure out if a smoking causes cancers, not correlation between a smoking and a person having lung cancer, but figuring out the causation between causation relationship between a variable and another variable or a response output, in those cases, we do causal inference. Again, not the topic for data 100, but just so that you know that everything that we're modeling in a stage one and two, it's mostly around finding a function that maps input to an output. But what we're looking at in the recent tree is to actually have a causal inference kind of argument and figuring out if a variable not only explains the output, but also causes the output. Again, others go pernata 100. Throughout the explanation of this reason one, two, three, I tried to say that interpretability is important and also accuracy is important. So what we'll try to do generally as data scientists is to strike the balance between interpretability and accuracy. So you really don't want to have a model, I mean, in some cases, we do have models that are not interpretable, but the best case scenario would be that If you have an intrapartibled model that is at the same time is accurate enough for you to use as well. So, common types of models. There are mechanistic models or deterministic models. These are models that generally follow a non-probabilistic view, meaning that there is a study or there are measurements, mechanistic measurements that we would do, such as, for example, the way that people arrived at Kepler's, for the law of planetary motion or Newton's law. And based on those measurements, they built a relationship between multiple different variables and they built those models. We call these models mechanistic or deterministic models. We call them deterministic because there is no probability involved. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

So you really don't want to have a model, I mean, in some cases, we do have models that are not interpretable, but the best case scenario would be that If you have an intrapartibled model that is at the same time is accurate enough for you to use as well. So, common types of models. There are mechanistic models or deterministic models. These are models that generally follow a non-probabilistic view, meaning that there is a study or there are measurements, mechanistic measurements that we would do, such as, for example, the way that people arrived at Kepler's, for the law of planetary motion or Newton's law. And based on those measurements, they built a relationship between multiple different variables and they built those models. We call these models mechanistic or deterministic models. We call them deterministic because there is no probability involved.

children.title: Deterministic vs. Probabilistic Models in Analysis
children.summary: Deterministic models are defined by laws that govern physical phenomena, such as Kepler's Third Law and Newton's Laws, demonstrating relationships like orbital periods and mass acceleration. In contrast, probabilistic models address the evolution of random processes, particularly in unpredictable systems, and are useful for classification tasks. The application of probabilistic models in brain wave analysis is highlighted, focusing on their role in predicting anxiety levels and seizure occurrences by analyzing brain activity data. This analysis aids in determining when to intensify patient monitoring based on the likelihood of seizures.

children.title: Common Types of Models: Deterministic and Probabilistic
children.summary: Deterministic physical models are based on laws that describe how the world operates, such as Kepler's Third Law of Planetary Motion and Newton's Laws of motion and gravitation, which illustrate relationships like the orbital period and mass acceleration. Probabilistic models, on the other hand, focus on how random processes evolve and are often used to understand unpredictable systems, exemplified by patient arrival times in a healthcare context.

children.title: Probabilistic Models in Brain Wave Analysis
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the use of probabilistic models to study brain waves and their correlation with anxiety levels and seizure prediction. It explains how these models analyze random processes in brain activity, helping to predict the likelihood of a seizure occurring based on recorded brain wave data over a specified period. If the probability of a seizure is deemed high, patient monitoring can be intensified in medical facilities.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So an example of that, I brought it here from one of my papers that is the work of my PhD student looking into studying brain waves in order to figure out the anxiety levels of subjects. So brain waves by themselves are random processes that you're modeling. There are probabilistic models that basically you will try to study and figure out how they travel in brain and how they interact with each other and how would they concentrate in a certain area so that it causes anxiety or it causes, for example, seizure. This is kind of the modeling that they would do in order to predict whether someone is going to have an onset of seizure in future or not just looking at those probabilistic models. And then you will basically have a probabilistic model that will say that with this specific likelihood, this subject that we had to recording up their brain waves over the last, like for example, seven days is going to have seizure in, for example, in the next 10 minutes. And if that probability is high enough then basically in patient facilities, they'll try to monitor that patient more accurately or more closely. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Categories of Models and Regression Line Derivation
children.summary: The discussion covers two main categories of models: mechanistic (deterministic) models that do not incorporate probability, and probabilistic models aimed at understanding random processes. It focuses on simple linear regression, providing a definition and deriving the regression line equation through calculus. The segment emphasizes the significance of derivatives and the optimization of objective functions, explaining what is expected from an objective function by the conclusion of the lecture.

children.title: Categories of Models and Regression Line Derivation
children.summary: The segment discusses two main categories of models: mechanistic (deterministic) models, which do not involve probability, and probabilistic models, which are used to understand random processes. The focus is on simple linear regression, including a definition and the derivation of the regression line equation using calculus. The importance of derivatives and optimizing objective functions is highlighted, with an explanation of what an objective function is expected by the end of the lecture.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is the kind of different categories of models that we're looking at. Mechanistic or deterministic, there's no probability involved and probabilistic models that again you will see later on in data 100. What we're going to do in data 100 right now as a starting point, just to get warmed up as to take a look at simple linear regression and what you'd see in data 8. So we're going to try go over regression line and the definition for regression line and actually derive the equation for regression line that you'd seen. But this time we're going to actually use a little bit of calculus in this lecture to just get warm up because you will need to get comfortable with actually doing derivatives and calculating or optimizing an objective function and whatnot. I'm going to tell you what objective function is by the end of today's lecture. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Regression Line Components and Estimation
children.summary: The regression line predicts the number of characters in chapters of 'Little Women' based on the number of periods in those chapters. The slope is calculated as the correlation (R) multiplied by the standard deviation of Y divided by the standard deviation of X, while the intercept is the average of Y minus the slope times the average of X. The regression line minimizes the mean squared error of estimation among all straight lines. Understanding the regression line involves deriving equations where the slope is calculated using the standard deviations and the correlation coefficient, and the intercept is determined similarly. An example illustrates estimating characters based on periods in the chapters.

children.title: Regression Line and Its Components
children.summary: The regression line predicts the number of characters in chapters of the novel 'Little Women' based on the number of periods in those chapters. The slope of the regression line is defined as the correlation (R) multiplied by the standard deviation of Y divided by the standard deviation of X. The intercept is calculated as the average of Y minus the slope times the average of X. The regression line minimizes the mean squared error of estimation among all straight lines.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So let's dive in. You've seen this regression line from before, right? So you've seen that in data aid, this was a famous plot that you had that was predicting on different chapters of little women novel that using a number of periods, period punctuations in a chapter, it was trying to predict the number of characters that would appear in that specific chapter. You would see that it tries to modulate using regression line and then we're going to take a look at the equations for that regression line and HUD data aid arrived at that regression line. You also see that in data aid, we defined a slope as correlation or R times the standard deviation of Y over the standard deviation of X. This is your Y dimension and this is your X dimension and correlation. I'm going to formula define it in a sec. An intercept, we talked about intercept or the point that when X is equal to zero, your Y value is non-zero probably, or that's called Y intercept or intercept was defined as average of Y minus slope times average of X. children.title: Understanding the Regression Line and Model Equations
children.summary: The regression line is defined as the unique straight line that minimizes the mean squared error of estimation among all straight lines. To derive all necessary equations for a model, only two key aspects need to be understood. The slope of the regression line is calculated using the standard deviation of y divided by the standard deviation of x, multiplied by the correlation coefficient (ry). The intercept is determined by taking the average of y and subtracting the product of the slope and the average of x. An example is provided where the number of characters in each chapter of 'Little Women' is estimated based on the number of periods in that chapter.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And we're going to see why that's the case. So you don't need to actually memorize all of these equations anymore. You just need to know two things about a model in order to drive all the equations yourself. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Understanding the Regression Line
children.summary: The regression line is defined as the unique straight line that minimizes the mean squared error of estimation among all straight lines. The slope of the regression line is calculated using the standard deviation of y divided by the standard deviation of x, multiplied by the correlation coefficient (ry). The intercept is determined by the average of y minus the product of the slope and the average of x. The regression estimate can be expressed as the intercept plus the slope multiplied by x. An example is provided where the number of characters in each chapter of 'Little Women' is estimated based on the number of periods in that chapter.

children.title: Regression Estimation and Residual Analysis
children.summary: Regression estimation involves using a model to predict values by plugging in specific inputs into an equation that includes an intercept and a slope. Residuals, defined as the difference between observed and estimated values, are crucial in assessing the accuracy of predictions. The goal in data modeling is to minimize these residuals across the dataset, indicating a better fit of the model to the data points. Some residuals may be positive or negative, reflecting the discrepancies between actual values and their projections onto the regression line. Further formal definitions of residuals will be provided later in the lecture.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We also talked about regression estimation. So regression estimation is that, okay, you have a model or estimation on any model, per se, is that we have a model and we're gonna just plug in the value that we wanna do prediction on into the equation and the equation was intercept plus slope times the value and then that would give you the regression estimation. There is a concept of residual that we're going to play a lot with and residual is the fine as observed value minus the estimated value. Okay, so the residuals are actually shown here on the plot. This is a residual. This is a residual. This is a residual and so on and so forth. What we're trying to do in data 100 and true out the modeling is that we're looking at the residuals that we're accumulating true out the entire data set and we're trying to minimize the amount of residual and I'm going to define what amount of residual means formally using a function mathematical function later on in today's lecture, but just know that if a point is not on the line, that's an imperfect prediction and we're going to measure how imperfect it is and we're going to try to make sure that the model that we're using is going to be a real we're fitting to the data is passing through all the data points as accurately as possible. One observation here is that some residuals are positive, some residuals are negative. What you're seeing here is that, for example, for this specific data point, the estimation is exactly this, sorry, the actual value is this value right here on the y-axis. And the estimated value is the projection of that point onto the line, and then you would see what that value is, right? Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Understanding Residuals and Regression Line
children.summary: The segment explains the concept of residuals in regression analysis, where 'hat' represents the predicted values. It describes how positive residuals occur for data points above the regression line and negative residuals for those below. The relationship between positive and negative residuals is highlighted, indicating that the regression line generally passes through the center of the data points. Additionally, the regression line is defined as the unique straight line that minimizes the mean squared error of estimation, with the equation involving slope and intercept derived from the averages of y and x.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is my ground truth why and this is why hat. I put hat as a representation of this is the predicted value or the estimated value. And you can see that based on this equation, the residual is a positive value for the points that are above the line. And you can also rationalize in the same way that for any point that's below the line, the residual is a negative value. You will see later on that there is a relationship between all of these residuals that are positive under residuals that are negative. And that's why the line generally passes through in the middle of the data point. So you have some positive residuals and some negative residuals. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

children.title: Correlation, Standardization, and Regression Analysis
children.summary: Correlation is defined as the average of the product of X and Y in standard units, which requires data to be zero-centered with a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one. Standardization involves calculating the average, centering the data by subtracting the mean, and dividing by the standard deviation. The segment explains the correlation between variables, noting that a positive correlation close to 1 indicates a strong linear association, while a negative correlation suggests a weaker relationship. It also highlights that a correlation near zero may imply a quadratic relationship, necessitating different modeling. Furthermore, it discusses transforming data back to original units in regression, where the regression line passes through the averages of x and y, and describes how the relationship between x and y is scaled. The equation for predicting y in non-standard units is presented, emphasizing the transition from standard to original units.

children.title: Understanding Correlation and Standardization
children.summary: Correlation is defined as the average of the product of X and Y when both are measured in a standard unit. A standard unit means the data is zero-centered with a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one. To standardize data, one calculates the average, subtracts it from each sample to center it, and then divides by the standard deviation to ensure the standard deviation equals one. The average of the products of X and Y in standard units is found by summing these products and dividing by the number of observations.

So if you need to refresh your please go back and take a look at data aid or just calmly look at the slides after the lecture and it will all make sense. What was correlation? So I want to break down the definition of correlation here for you. The definition from data aid textbook says that correlation is the average of the product of X and Y. When both are measured in a standard unit. Right? Three pieces of information. And let me tell you what they are. It's starting from measuring in a standard unit. When we say that a variable is an standard unit, that means that it's zero center to center deviation of one. Right? So the average of all the values should be zero. And then the standard deviation of all of the values should be equal to one. And the way that we actually standardize a data is to go through this process right here, that you take one column of values or one set of values. You calculate their average, you subtract the average from each individual sample. So that's how you will make sure that it's zero centered, meaning that the mean is at zero. And then in order to make sure that the standard deviation is equal to one, you divide all of these transformations by standard deviation of that entire column or that set of values. This ensures that the standard deviation is equal to one as well. So that's what we mean by a standardization. So that's a standard unit. So when we say product of X and Y, both measured in a standard unit, this means that X measured in a standard unit is right here. Y measured in a standard unit is right here. So that's X and Y measured in a standard unit. And then there was an additional piece that says that the average of those products, and you're seeing that here I'm taking average by summing up all of these products and then dividing it by one over n, which is the average, right? So what I need to do is go through each one of those rows, zero center or standardize all of those values. Once all of those values are standardized, I will basically try to calculate the product of the two x standard unit, y standard unit in each given row, and then I have a value. When correlation is equal to zero, it shows that we have a weak linear association. It doesn't tell us that there is no association between them. This ensures that the standard deviation is equal to one as well. So that's what we mean by a standardization. So that's a standard unit. children.title: Correlation and Linear Relationships in Data
children.summary: The segment discusses the correlation between variables x and y, highlighting different types of relationships. A significant positive correlation close to 1 indicates a strong linear association, while a negative correlation of -0.7 suggests a weaker linear association with some noise in the data. It emphasizes that a correlation close to zero may indicate a quadratic relationship rather than a linear one, which requires different modeling approaches. Additionally, it explains that in simple linear regression with zero-centered data, the regression line passes through the origin, affecting the interpretation of correlation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: it looks like that is randomly distributed. So that's why you're seeing that the correlation is also a very small value. Here in this case, we're seeing a significant positive correlation or a linear association between x and y. That's why it's also shown by this value that's very close to 1. In the third case, what you're seeing is that as we're increasing x, the y decreases, right? Roughly y decreases. There's a little bit of noise obviously, but what we're expecting is that we're expecting to have some linear association, but the linear association is negative. And the valid digesting here is negative 0.7. And comparing negative points 7 with point 9.5 that we had in the second case, you were seeing that the linear association is this slightly weaker because we have some spread around some spread in the data or some noise in the data that is kind of keeping us from having a perfect linear association. But despite that, that issue we still see some linear association. In the last case, what would you guess the correlation be? The correlation, so this is kind of where I'm going to try to re-emphasize that what we're seeing in this case is that there is some relationship between x and y, right? What we're seeing in fact is a quadratic relationship. But since it's not a linear relationship, if you go ahead and calculate correlation, it's going to give us a correlation of close to zero. Because again, there is no linear pattern between x and y. But there is non-linear pattern that can be captured using other methods or other kinds of modeling that we can do. So moving on and kind of talking about what does it mean when we calculate correlation? When we have correlation of R in a simple linear regression data, simple linear regression word, and when our data is zero centered, meaning that when our data is zero centered, our regression line is going to pass through the origin. And then we talk about the correlation. That means that by every one unit increase in X, the Y value is going to increase by our unit. And again, notice that if R is positive, that means that it's an increasing trend, when R is negative, that means that that's a decreasing trend, that's a negative slope, and that's how you can make sense of what's happening in the regression kind of line. children.title: Transforming Data to Original Units in Regression
children.summary: The discussion focuses on converting data points back to their original units rather than using standard units. It explains that the regression line will pass through the averages of x and y instead of the origin due to adjustments made by removing the averages. The relationship between x and y is scaled appropriately, where for each standard deviation unit of x, y changes by r times the standard deviation of y. The equation presented shows the prediction for y in non-standard units, emphasizing the transition from standard to original units.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But we don't want to live in the standard unit word. What we want to live in is in the original unit or the original kind of composition of the data set that we had. So what we're going to do is we're going to move back all of our data all of our data sets or data points into the stand into the original units that they were meaning that our data point or our. A regression line is going to pass through average of x and average of y instead of passing through the origin. Right. Again, because we removed the, removed average of x from all of the x values and removed average of y from all the y values. When we added back, then it will basically push all of the data points by a factor of average of x and average of y. And that's why the origin is going to move to the point that corresponds to average of x and average of y. What you're seeing here is that x is the scale appropriately. Here we would say that for every standard deviation unit of x, y is going to be changed by r times the standard deviation of y. Right? That's how, again, you're scaling across the x, a scaling across the y because previously you divided by standard deviation of x and a standard deviation of y. If you multiply that back, this is what you're going to get. One is going to be multiplied by standard deviation of x, and R is going to be multiplied by standard deviation of y. That's why this scale is going to be different. So the equation that we will have, and this is the equation that you had seen in data 8 is that what we have is the equation for this line is y hat or the prediction for y minus y bar divided by a standard deviation of y. This is kind of moving all of these, all of the points or all of the equations from the top equation down to the bottom equation, but moving it to non-standard unit. So when I had y hat before, I'm going to here say that this y hat was measured in a standard unit, but the non-standard unit is going to be this y hat. So y hat minus average of y divided by standard division of x, I'm moving r down and then I'm also saying that the way that I calculated x in the standard unit was that x minus x bar divided by standard division of x. That's the equation that we'll be working with. If I move this equation to this address slide and try to simplify and just make sure that why hat remains on one side and then everything else is going to be remained on the right hand side and then I'm basically trying to get the coefficient, get a hat which is your intercept and also be hat which was your slope then we can basically figure out what is the the interest of equation and what is the, what is this little equation in our case. So let me start doing that. From this point and on, I might write equations on this slide, but when I publish this slide, the slides are going to have animations so you can actually walk through the animation and figure out all the steps. So what I'm going to do in order to simplify this equation is that I'm going to multiply both sides by standard deviation of y so that I can get the rate of the denominator in the left hand side. So what I'll have is y hat minus y bar is equal to r times the standard deviation of y multiplied by. x minus x bar divided by standard deviation of x. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Isolating Coefficients in Regression Equation
children.summary: The discussion focuses on rearranging the regression equation to isolate the coefficient associated with the variable x. The equation is reformulated to express y hat as a function of r times the standard deviation of y divided by the standard deviation of x, multiplied by x, plus y bar. The goal is to have a clear separation of the variable term and the constant term in the regression line equation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Right. my goal is always to have this form, right? In order to get to that form, I just need to move also y bar to the other side of the equation. So what I'll have is that y hat is going to be equal to r times the standard deviation of y divided by a standard deviation of x multiplied by x minus x bar plus y bar. Right. So so far so good. The only thing that now I need to do is to isolate everything that is multiplied with x into one coefficient that I want to use as B hat, right? So I want to have one term that has all the things or all the coefficients that are multiplied with the variable and every constant term that's going to be added to this core term is going to be separate. So meaning that I want to actually split and take out x from this, parenthesis or this equation right here, which will tell me that why had this going to be equal to our times the standard deviation of y divided by standard deviation of x times x, right? And then the other thing that I'll have is Y bar. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Slope of Regression Line and Its Calculation
children.summary: The slope of the regression line, represented as b hat, is derived from the formula R times the standard deviation of Y divided by the standard deviation of X. This relationship was previously established in Data 8, emphasizing that the regression line equation is defined as y = â + bx, where the slope can be calculated using the correlation coefficient R.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Minus are times the standard deviation of y divided by standard deviation of x times x bar. Right? I just rearranged some of the turns. So if we go ahead and take a look at what we had, the coefficient that's multiplied by x is going to be my slope or is going to be my b hat, which that coefficient right here is exactly what we're seeing right here. So this slope is going to be, I'm going to write it here, that this slope is going to be equal to R times the standard division of Y divided by standard division of X. This is what we had seen before. We just gave it to you in data 8 that this slope is equal to R times the standard division of Y divided by standard division of X. children.title: Understanding the Regression Line Equation
children.summary: The regression line equation is defined as y = â + b(x - x̄), where the slope is represented by r(y,x) times the standard deviation of y over the standard deviation of x. This segment discusses the correlation, the standard unit, and how the shape of the regression line is determined by increments in x affecting y. Key components include the slope and intercept, with emphasis on the relationship between the variables.

children.title: Regression Line Equation and Residuals
children.summary: The equation for the slope of the regression line is discussed, where y bar minus the slope times x bar represents the predicted value. The residual or error in prediction for a data point is defined as the actual value minus the predicted value (y hat). This segment emphasizes the relationship between the slope and the residuals in regression analysis.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I'm going to write that out that this is y bar minus. What you're noticing here is that this term right here is exactly what I had for this slope. So I can directly say that that term is equal to slope. So I have y bar minus slope. Times. Explore. And that was the equation for the slope that we had from before. That the slope is always y bar minus r times y bar minus a slope times x bar. And then obviously, what we have for the residual is that y residual or the error in prediction for data point i is equal to the exact value minus the predicted value y i hat. hat and I use hat because that's a predicted value and that's not the exact value that we calculate. children.title: Deriving the Regression Line Equation
children.summary: The lecture focuses on deriving the regression line equation using calculus. The regression line is defined by the equation y = a + b*x, where 'a' is the intercept and 'b' is the slope. The error for each data point is represented as Ci = yi - Gi. The goal is to derive and explain all components of the regression line equation presented in the slides.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then why I had you can actually derive it using this equation which now we derive both this lobe and interest of it we can directly have the equation of the line and actually go ahead and calculate it. So the goal that I have for today's lecture is actually go ahead and derive all of these equations one more time using calculus and then we'll see how we can actually do that. children.title: Modeling Process and Variable Definitions
children.summary: The modeling process starts with a dataset that includes attributes or inputs used to predict a target variable (Y). Each observation has features (X) and a corresponding target value (Y). Simple linear regression involves one input feature predicting a single target variable, while multiple regression uses multiple input features for one target value. Predictions are represented as y hats (ŷ), with each observation providing a predicted value.

children.title: Modeling Process and Variable Definitions
children.summary: The modeling process begins with a dataset containing one or more attributes or inputs that are used to predict a target variable (Y). Each observation in the dataset consists of features or attributes (X) and a corresponding target value (Y). In simple linear regression, there is one input feature and one target variable, while multiple regression involves multiple input features to predict a single target value. Predictions are denoted as y hats (ŷ), with each observation yielding a predicted value.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And what's the formal way of us defining how good of a model, how good a model can be and how we can objectively evaluate it. So let's go through the modeling process. So this is the process that we will go over every time that we want to build a model. Up to the end of the data 100 lecture, every time that I want to talk about creating a new model, I'm going to follow this same modeling process. And let me tell you what this modeling process involves. The first step of the modeling process is that every time that we start doing a modeling, there is going to be a data set. Your data set is going to have one or more attributes or inputs that we're predicting, and then it's always going to have a Y or a target variable that we're trying to match it to. So there. Each row of our data frame that we're providing you is going to be an observation, which one piece of that is going to have inputs, features or attributes that we're going to model in the simple linear regression board. We're assuming that there is only one input that we're trying to match to the target. And then the second instance is always going to be output outcome or target variable. So we're trying to use X eyes in order to predict Y eyes as accurately as possible. Again, in simple linear regression, we're only concerned with having one feature or one input, and then we always have one target value. But when we would move to multiple regression, that's the case that we will have multiple different features, and we're trying to combine them together in order to create, in order to predict the output or target variable. But always the target variable is going to be a single value for every row of observation that we might have. Every time that we're referring to the fact that we're doing the prediction, we're going to say that the predictions are going to be y hats. For every row, there is going to be a y-i hat that we're predicting. Again, here I have n rows. So the predictions that I have is going to be y-i, y-1 hat, y-2 hats, and then so on and so forth until y-n hat. In terms of the models that we will build today and also in the next couple of lectures, we're going to start by building a simple linear regression. So, a simple linear regression is going as having equation like this, that there's always an intercept term, tetasera, and then there's always the slope that we're always referring to that as teta1 times input, and then we will combine the two terms in order to get the production. We will also see other types of models in terms of lecture and also next Tuesday that is going to be constant model and multiple linear regression that we'll talk about them later. I want to put out there that all the models that we'll be discussing, they're called parametric models. Because using the data, we will learn parameters of the model, all of those theta values are parameters that once we know those parameters and once we know what kind of model we use, then we can directly do the estimation of each one, or the calculation of each one of the estimated values as accurately as possible. So these are the parameters that we use for the modeling purposes and that's why there are parametric models. When we say that we have all of these parametric models, the only parameters that we have are all of those slow-band interest step and those coefficient values. Everything else is going to be input or the features that we're using. So in other words, the x and y values are not our parameters. So only the TETA values are our parameters. When I say that I use a parametric model that is parametrized by the TETA value? Then I will go ahead and drive what is the optimum set of TETA values that is going to be as accurately as possible model through my entire data set. We call them TETA hats. Hat on top of TETA means that there was a model that now this TETA parameter for that model is the optimum set of parameters. And I'm going to show you what that means if a model is not optimum, because technically when you define a model parameter, or when you're defining a parametric model, you're just defining a function or a mathematical function. You're not trying to find out what is the optimum mathematical function that models your entire dataset. So this is what it means. When we have a model, we're basically trying to have, could define the family of functions that can be used in order to model our relationship. We would say that, okay, if I have the model parameters to be theta, then my model equation is going to be y hat equal to teta 0 plus teta 1 times x. When I say that I have the best optimum teta value, I will go ahead and put a hat on top of it, which basically means that this time, if you go ahead and calculate y hat values using these optimum values, this is the best prediction that you can have an average on the entire data set that we have. Again, this right here is going to be a family of the models that can be applied to our data set, but there is only one set of values that is tetA0 hadn't tetA1 had that are going to be optimal. Okay, and that's that that's where what we're going to be after. In data 100, one thing that you notice is that we're moving away from using A and B for linear regression and we're moving into the TETA 0 and TETA 1 ward. And that's very important. We're not using TETA 0 and TETA 1 because we think we're fancy, but this is because what I can do is that if I keep adding features or attribute to my model, then what I can have is that I can have a coefficient or the theta value associated with each one of those attributes, and I can combine all of them into a vector. So for example, in this case, what I've done is that I'm saying that my model uses theta zero and theta one. Let's combine them into a vector of theta. And you pass me theta or you pass me theta hat. leaf_chunk.audio: And what's the formal way of us defining how good of a model, how good a model can be and how we can objectively evaluate it. So let's go through the modeling process. So this is the process that we will go over every time that we want to build a model. Up to the end of the data 100 lecture, every time that I want to talk about creating a new model, I'm going to follow this same modeling process. And let me tell you what this modeling process involves. The first step of the modeling process is that every time that we start doing a modeling, there is going to be a data set. Your data set is going to have one or more attributes or inputs that we're predicting, and then it's always going to have a Y or a target variable that we're trying to match it to. So there. Each row of our data frame that we're providing you is going to be an observation, which one piece of that is going to have inputs, features or attributes that we're going to model in the simple linear regression board. We're assuming that there is only one input that we're trying to match to the target. And then the second instance is always going to be output outcome or target variable. So we're trying to use X eyes in order to predict Y eyes as accurately as possible. Again, in simple linear regression, we're only concerned with having one feature or one input, and then we always have one target value. But when we would move to multiple regression, that's the case that we will have multiple different features, and we're trying to combine them together in order to create, in order to predict the output or target variable. But always the target variable is going to be a single value for every row of observation that we might have. Every time that we're referring to the fact that we're doing the prediction, we're going to say that the predictions are going to be y hats. For every row, there is going to be a y-i hat that we're predicting. Again, here I have n rows. So the predictions that I have is going to be y-i, y-1 hat, y-2 hats, and then so on and so forth until y-n hat. In terms of the models that we will build today and also in the next couple of lectures, we're going to start by building a simple linear regression. So, a simple linear regression is going as having equation like this, that there's always an intercept term, tetasera, and then there's always the slope that we're always referring to that as teta1 times input, and then we will combine the two terms in order to get the production. We will also see other types of models in terms of lecture and also next Tuesday that is going to be constant model and multiple linear regression that we'll talk about them later. I want to put out there that all the models that we'll be discussing, they're called parametric models. Because using the data, we will learn parameters of the model, all of those theta values are parameters that once we know those parameters and once we know what kind of model we use, then we can directly do the estimation of each one, or the calculation of each one of the estimated values as accurately as possible. So these are the parameters that we use for the modeling purposes and that's why there are parametric models. When we say that we have all of these parametric models, the only parameters that we have are all of those slow-band interest step and those coefficient values. Everything else is going to be input or the features that we're using. So in other words, the x and y values are not our parameters. So only the TETA values are our parameters. When I say that I use a parametric model that is parametrized by the TETA value? Then I will go ahead and drive what is the optimum set of TETA values that is going to be as accurately as possible model through my entire data set. We call them TETA hats. Hat on top of TETA means that there was a model that now this TETA parameter for that model is the optimum set of parameters. And I'm going to show you what that means if a model is not optimum, because technically when you define a model parameter, or when you're defining a parametric model, you're just defining a function or a mathematical function. You're not trying to find out what is the optimum mathematical function that models your entire dataset. So this is what it means. When we have a model, we're basically trying to have, could define the family of functions that can be used in order to model our relationship. We would say that, okay, if I have the model parameters to be theta, then my model equation is going to be y hat equal to teta 0 plus teta 1 times x. When I say that I have the best optimum teta value, I will go ahead and put a hat on top of it, which basically means that this time, if you go ahead and calculate y hat values using these optimum values, this is the best prediction that you can have an average on the entire data set that we have. Again, this right here is going to be a family of the models that can be applied to our data set, but there is only one set of values that is tetA0 hadn't tetA1 had that are going to be optimal. Okay, and that's that that's where what we're going to be after. In data 100, one thing that you notice is that we're moving away from using A and B for linear regression and we're moving into the TETA 0 and TETA 1 ward. And that's very important. We're not using TETA 0 and TETA 1 because we think we're fancy, but this is because what I can do is that if I keep adding features or attribute to my model, then what I can have is that I can have a coefficient or the theta value associated with each one of those attributes, and I can combine all of them into a vector. children.title: Parametric Models and Parameter Estimation Overview
children.summary: Parametric models are defined by a limited number of parameters, such as 00 and 01, which are informed by data rather than predetermined. The x and y values are directly observed and not considered parameters. The sample-based estimate of a parameter is denoted as ê, distinguishing it from the 'hat' notation used in predictions. The segment contrasts parametric models with non-parametric models, like Kernel Density Estimates (KDEs), which do not rely on specific parameters but estimate values based on data. It is emphasized that not all statistical models have parameters, with examples including KDEs and k-Nearest Neighbor classifiers. A linear model is used to illustrate how parameters fit the model to the data.

children.title: Parametric Models and Parameter Estimation
children.summary: Parametric models are characterized by a limited number of parameters, such as 00 and 01, which are informed by the data rather than provided directly. The x and y values are not considered parameters since they are directly observed. The sample-based estimate of a parameter is denoted as ê, distinguishing it from the 'hat' used in predictions. Typically, the parameters selected are those that yield the 'best' fit according to a chosen criterion, exemplified by a linear model with parameters 0 = [00, 01].

children.title: Parametric vs Non-parametric Models
children.summary: Parametric models are characterized by a limited number of parameters, such as 00 and 01, which are informed by the data rather than predetermined. In contrast, non-parametric models, like Kernel Density Estimates (KDEs), do not rely on specific parameters but estimate values based on the data. The segment also discusses the notation for parameters and the distinction between estimated parameters (denoted with a hat, ê) and observed values.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Instead of just go to a, b, c, d, and so on and so forth. this way just using the subscript of i, tetasero, tetah1, tetah2, and so on and so forth. What we can do is we can consolidate them into a tetavector and then we can index into the relationship between each feature has what weight more accurately or more easily. So that's kind of the notation that we'll be using. I said here we have a parametric model. We have seen non parametric models before. So some statistical models do not have parameters. For example, we have seen KDE's. KDE's were models, or kernel density estimate. But we did not set any parameters. We would just estimate alpha, and then we would just run a process, and we would have KDE's. Video: 1: lec10.ipynb - JupyterLab

children.title: Parametric vs Non-parametric Models
children.summary: Parametric models are defined by a limited number of parameters, which are informed by the data rather than predetermined. The x and y values are observed directly and not considered parameters. The estimated parameter, denoted as ê, indicates a sample-based estimate, while the 'hat' notation differentiates between estimates and predictions. It's noted that not all statistical models have parameters, with examples of non-parametric models including Kernel Density Estimates (KDEs) and k-Nearest Neighbor classifiers. A linear model is presented as an example, where parameters are used to fit the model to the data.

children.title: Introduction to Parametric Models in Regression
children.summary: Parametric models are characterized by a limited number of parameters, which are informed by the data rather than being predefined. In this lecture, the focus is on learning to optimally derive these parameters for creating models, specifically starting with simple linear regression. The process of model building begins with selecting the model category, and while today's focus is on linear regression, other models will be explored in future lectures. It's noted that not all statistical models have parameters, with examples of non-parametric models like KDEs and k-Nearest Neighbor classifiers provided.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So those are the cases that there are models that are non-parametric, there are models that are parametric, but we're dealing with is parametric models. And what we're going to do throughout this multiple lecture is to learn those parameters, how to optimally learn those parameters, how to derive those parameters so that we can uniquely have our own model to work with. So the terminology and we know the notation that we'll be using, let me walk you through the modeling process. Every time that we want to build a model, irrespective of what model you're building, you first need to choose the category of model that you want to use. So for example, for today's lecture, what we would say is that our model is going to be a simple linear regression. We might change our mind in the next few lectures, and we will keep using different types of models. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Steps in the Modeling Process
children.summary: The modeling process involves three main steps: 1) selecting a model to represent the world, 2) choosing a loss function to quantify prediction error, and 3) fitting the model by selecting optimal parameters based on data. The loss function measures the alignment of predictions with actual outcomes and its optimization is crucial in the modeling process.

children.title: Steps in the Modeling Process
children.summary: The modeling process consists of three main steps: 1) Choose a model to represent the world, 2) Choose a loss function to quantify prediction error, and 3) Fit the model by selecting the best parameters based on the data. The loss function is defined as a measure of how well the predictions align with the actual outcomes, and its optimization is a key part of the modeling process.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But the first step is that you figure out what framework you want to use. The second step is to choose a loss function. What is a loss function? Loss function is a function that will tell you how good your bad your predictions are. And I'm going to objectively define what this loss function means and how to choose a loss function in the next few slides. Once we have the model and we have the loss function, we'll go through an optimization process. This is a fun part that we'll actually go ahead and try to optimize our loss function in a way that we find the best set of parameters for our model. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Model Evaluation and Simple Linear Regression Overview
children.summary: The segment covers the transition from TETA to TETA hats in model evaluation, emphasizing the importance of assessing the model after fitting with optimal parameters. Simple linear regression (SLR) is introduced, detailing the notation and terminology, including the TETA vector for predicting outputs. It discusses the model's parameters, theta 0 and theta 1, and introduces the loss function, which measures prediction accuracy against target values. The process of defining and optimizing a cost function to find the best parameters is explained, along with the acknowledgment that true relationships are often non-linear. An example involving the estimation of characters in 'Little Women' based on periods in chapters is presented, prompting participants to suggest optimal parameter values.

children.title: Model Evaluation and Simple Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the transition from TETA to TETA hats in the modeling process, emphasizing the importance of evaluating the model after fitting it with optimum parameters. It introduces simple linear regression as the chosen model for this lecture, explaining the new notation and terminology. The TETA vector is described as a means to find a family of functions, where input values lead to predictions. The focus is on finding optimal parameters that yield the best estimations, indicated by the use of hats on estimations and TETA.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we go from having TETA up to having TETA hats. And once we have TETA hats, we have our model, we have optimized model, and we're ready to go. Once we have our TETA hat and we fit the model and we find the optimum parameters, we need to evaluate the model. So we're going to briefly talk about whether the evaluation criteria that we'll have after we have the model to make sure that our model is performing well on the data set that we have. So let's start with choosing a model, which should be simple in this case. We're choosing simple linear regression for the purpose of today's lecture, because today's lecture is just basically setting you up with all the new notation and the new terminology that we're using. We're going from AMB, we're going into the word of TETA, so we're using TETA 0 as my Y interest of and TETA 1 as this loop that we're using. So what I said was that this tetah vector that we have is the finding a family of functions. So what that means is that if you are saying that you have a tetah vector and you pass the values of tetasurant, tetah one of that tetah vector, you pass the input, you have the predictions. So think about that as a machine that throughout the modeling process will try to find the parameters for this machine that gives us the best estimations, right? So that's what it means. What we have is that the parameters are sitting in the middle and we're trying to find the optimums that are parameters in the middle to get the best estimations, the best estimations as much as possible. So one thing to notice here is that I'm using also hat on top of the estimations. And I was using hat one more time when I was putting it on top of tetas. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Simple Linear Regression and Loss Function
children.summary: The segment discusses the basics of simple linear regression (SLR), explaining that the model is defined by two parameters, theta 0 and theta 1, which are used to predict the output variable y (denoted as y hat). It introduces the concept of a loss function, which quantifies how well the model's predictions match the target values. The process involves defining a cost function and optimizing it to find the best parameters (theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat) for the model, acknowledging that the true relationship between x and y is often non-linear.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This is kind of operate or overloading that I'm using. So when it happens on top of why that means that's an estimation. When hat is on top of, or that's the prediction. When hat is on top of the teta, that means that that's the optimum set of parameters for the model that I'm using. The first step is done. We have a linear, simple linear regression. We know that the pattern of the function is going to be that two parameters are going to be given, and then we can calculate the prediction of y, which is y hat, using teta 0 plus theta 1 times x. Now we need to get into what is the loss function? And how can we define how good we are matching to the target values based on different models that we have? So we are going to actually follow a certain process that defining a cost function and optimizing the cost function up to the point that we get to the optimum set of parameters that I'm referring to as theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat. So choosing a loss function, how can we quantify how good or bad a function is map to our data set? Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Evaluating Model Accuracy in Linear Models
children.summary: The discussion focuses on evaluating the accuracy of different linear models based on their fit to a data set. It is noted that the blue line is more accurate than the red line because it minimizes the average distance of data points from the line, leading to lower error. The concept of measuring accuracy through miscalculations and distances from the lines is emphasized, encouraging consideration of optimal parameters for the linear model.

children.title: Model Accuracy and Data Representation
children.summary: The discussion focuses on evaluating the accuracy of different linear models based on their fit to a data set. The blue line is identified as more accurate than the red line, as it minimizes the average distance of data points from the line, resulting in lower error. The concept of measuring accuracy through miscalculations and distances from the lines is emphasized, prompting consideration of optimal parameters for the linear model.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then I draw all of those lines. So what we're wondering is that just looking at the shape of these lines and the distribution of the data, which one of these models are more accurate. And then think about how you're going to measure in your mind what accuracy means. This is your first slide of the day, which one of these lines matches the data set more accurately. OK, it's kind of clear that the blue line is matching our data said more accurately. And the process that probably went in your mind was that, OK, I'm looking at the miscalculations or misproductions. I'm looking at how far the points are from each one of those lines. And it looks like that when I'm looking at the distances from the line, the blue line has the minimum distance on average across all of the across all of the points in a data set versus, for example, if you're looking at the red line, if you want to calculate the distances of all of the points from the line, that's a very high error that we're accumulating throughout the entire data set. We need some metric of how "good" or "bad" our predictions are.
1380664
A loss function characterizes the cost, error, or fit resulting from a particular choice of model
or model parameters.
Loss quantifies how bad a prediction is for a single observation.
If our prediction y is close to the actual value y, we want low loss.
L(y, ŷ)
If our prediction y is far from the actual value y, we want high loss.

children.title: Understanding and Comparing L1 and L2 Loss Functions
children.summary: The segment covers the concept of loss functions in predictions, emphasizing their role in quantifying the difference between predicted and actual values. It highlights the importance of selecting appropriate loss functions based on the task type, such as classification or regression, and discusses the critical implications in applications like cancer identification. The segment details L1 and L2 loss functions, explaining L2 loss as squared loss and L1 loss as absolute loss, with examples illustrating how these losses are calculated in simple linear regression. Formulas for both loss types are provided, reinforcing their utility in evaluating model predictions.

children.title: Understanding Loss Functions in Predictions
children.summary: The loss function quantifies the difference between predicted values (y hat) and actual values (y), assigning a low loss for close predictions and a high loss for distant ones. This function is crucial as it penalizes the model for miscalculations, with the choice of loss function varying based on the type of task, such as classification versus regression. Different loss functions are necessary for different applications, like cancer identification, where missing a positive case is critical. The course will cover multiple loss functions and their implications for model evaluation.

We need some metric of how "good" or "bad" our predictions are.
1380664
A loss function characterizes the cost, error, or fit resulting from a particular choice of model
or model parameters.
Loss quantifies how bad a prediction is for a single observation.
If our prediction y is close to the actual value y, we want low loss.
L(y,ý)
If our prediction y is far from the actual value y, we want high loss.
There are many definitions of loss functions!
The choice of loss function:
Affects the accuracy and computational cost of estimation.
Depends on the estimation task:
Are outputs quantitative or qualitative?

children.title: L1 and L2 Loss in Modeling
children.summary: L2 loss, also known as squared loss, calculates the square of the residual, while L1 loss, or absolute loss, considers the absolute value of the residual. For example, with a residual of 100 units, L1 loss remains 100 units, indicating that a good prediction occurs when the predicted value matches the actual value, resulting in no loss. Conversely, significant deviations from the actual value lead to increased loss, highlighting the importance of accurate predictions in modeling.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's kind of L2 loss or the second order loss that we have. The absolute loss just looks at the residual and calculus the absolute value. Again, meaning that if it's the smallest, it's just going to be that same absolute value of the residual. If it's 100 unit of residual, it's just going to be same 100 units. children.title: L1 and L2 Loss in Simple Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses L1 and L2 loss functions used in modeling, specifically in the context of simple linear regression (SLR). L2 loss, also known as squared loss, and L1 loss, or absolute loss, are both positive values representing cost, indicating that a good prediction results in no loss, while a bad prediction incurs some loss. The formulas for the losses are presented as L(y,g) = (y - g)^2 for L2 loss and L(y,g) = |y - g| for L1 loss. The segment emphasizes that both loss types are widely used and reasonable for evaluating model predictions.

children.title: Understanding L1 and L2 Loss in Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the application of L1 and L2 loss functions in linear regression. It explains how to calculate the loss for individual data points using these functions, emphasizing that L2 loss (squared loss) penalizes larger residuals more than smaller ones, making it ideal for situations where large errors need to be heavily penalized. However, a drawback of using squared loss is its sensitivity to outliers, which can distort the model's fit by shifting the regression line away from the actual trend to minimize overall loss.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what does it mean in the... in the language of linear regression, that means that I'm going to go ahead and replace in every instance that I have y hat, I'm going to replace it with the equation for the simple linear regression. So here I have y minus y hat, so here my loss for simple linear regression based on L2 or a squared loss is going to be y minus this right here is y hat and then the squared. Same thing for the L1 loss, we just plug in y hat and then we have the equation. Note to step what I have right here is the loss or the cost or a single data point. But what we want is we want to have a line that matches all of the data sets, all of the data points in the data set as closely as possible. So in that case, what we would do is we would go ahead and take average over all of the data points. So let's go through two more slider questions to make sure that we're all on the same page with respect to What is L1 loss, what is L2 loss and so on and so forth? So the question is if you want to penalize large residuals more than the small residuals or proportionally, which loss function is more ideal?
