children.title: Importance of Positive Residuals in Cost Calculation
children.summary: The discussion explains why actual residual values cannot be used directly in calculations, as they include both positive and negative values that would cancel each other out when summed. To avoid this issue, absolute loss or squared loss is employed to ensure all cost values are positive. The process involves calculating the loss for each data point, summing these loss values, and then dividing by the number of data points (n) to obtain the average. This approach allows for a fair comparison of model performance across different datasets, ensuring that dataset size does not influence the evaluation of models. The cost function used for training the model is noted to be parametric, depending on parameters denoted by theta.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What about this question? Why don't we use the actual residual values directly? Why do we score them? Why do we calculate absolute value? Why do we want all of them to be all of the cost values to be positive? Why do we want to get rid of negatives? Sum will be zero. Residual sum to zero, that is correct. Um... Okay, they don't cancel out. So that seems to be the right trend. But the issue that we have when we try to just use the actual residual values is that some residual values are positive, some residual values are negative, right? This is what we saw before. And if we just actually get the residual values and sum them up, the issue is going to be there, going to cancel each other out. And again, we need to calculate the cost and aggregate in the entire data sets. So that's why we really want to make sure that all of them are positive cost values. And that's why we're using either absolute loss or a squared loss in all of these cases. I said we need to make sure that we calculate the loss for all of the data points. In fact, the way that we do it is that we will go ahead and sum up all of these loss values for all of the data points. So you see why I, why I have. That means that the prediction for data point I and assuming that we have n different data points, we will sum up all of those loss values that we have and then we will divide it by n, which is going to be calculating the average. Right? Summing up makes sense because I want to take a look at the entire data set. When I'm dividing it by n, this is the case that if I'm comparing the model performances on different data sets, I don't want the sides of the data sets to be influencing my decision about which model is performing better, right? Or which model is performing? We'll object the function that we'll be using in order to train a model and find the optimum set of parameters that we have. One note is that what you notice is that this function is a parametric function. It's parametrized by theta, meaning that if you give me different theta values here, I'm going to give you different y-hats. And by having different y-hats, you're going to have different losses for the data points. And then once you have different losses, you're going to have a different overall cost or the overall average loss. So, kind of notice that this function, depending on the teta is going to change, and our goal in this case is going to be to find the teta value or the set of teta zero and teta one values that minimizes this cost function that goes through all of the data points as closely as possible on average and have the minimum average residual values that we have. Okay, does that make sense? So this cost function is a really important cost function. children.title: Empirical Risk, Loss Functions, and MSE Minimization
children.summary: Empirical risk measures the average loss across a dataset, defined as the average of the loss function applied to each data point. For L2 loss, this is represented as mean squared error (MSE), calculated from the squared differences between predicted and actual values. In regression, using L2 loss focuses on MSE to minimize errors, known as least squares regression, which seeks to find optimal parameters (theta 0 and theta 1) that minimize MSE through derivative calculations. The segment highlights the role of calculus and linear algebra in minimizing MSE, introducing partial derivatives to find optimal values by setting them to zero, establishing the necessary conditions for regression analysis.

children.title: Empirical Risk and Loss Functions Explained
children.summary: Empirical risk measures the average loss across a dataset, evaluating how well a model's predictions fit the data. It is defined as the average of the loss function applied to each data point. For L2 loss, the empirical risk is calculated as the mean squared error (MSE), which is the average of the squared differences between predicted and actual values. Conversely, using L1 loss results in the average of the absolute residuals. The choice of loss function affects the empirical risk calculation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So think about this function as a function that evaluates the entire family of the lines that can pass through your data set. So when we go ahead and plug in for that loss function and plug in L2 loss, you can see that effectively the empirical risk is going to be 1 over N times the summation of Yi minus Yi i2, and we call that mean a squared error. And if you plug in L1 loss instead of this loss function into the empirical risk equation, this is going to give us 1 over N times the summation of absolute value of all of those residual values that we have. leaf_chunk.audio: So think about this function as a function that evaluates the entire family of the lines that can pass through your data set. So when we go ahead and plug in for that loss function and plug in L2 loss, you can see that effectively the empirical risk is going to be 1 over N times the summation of Yi minus Yi i2, and we call that mean a squared error. And if you plug in L1 loss instead of this loss function into the empirical risk equation, this is going to give us 1 over N times the summation of absolute value of all of those residual values that we have.

children.title: L2 Loss and Mean Squared Error in Regression
children.summary: The focus is on using L2 loss, specifically mean squared error (MSE), as the empirical risk function in simple linear regression. MSE is calculated by taking the square of the residuals for each data point. This method is referred to as least squares regression, as it aims to minimize the squared errors across the dataset. The discussion emphasizes the importance of fitting the model by finding optimal parameters (theta 0 and theta 1) that minimize the MSE, which involves calculating the derivative of the function.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, MSc versus MAE. In the next lecture, I'm going to come back and play with optimizing both of these functions. And I'm going to show you what's the difference between the final model that we'll get depending on which loss function that we use and which empirical response we get at the end. For today's purpose, what I'm going to do is I'm going to say that I'm going to use L2 loss, meaning that I'm going to go ahead and calculate the residual square of each one of those data points in the dataset. And my entire empirical risk function is going to be MSE or mean squared error. Again, this is the mean and this is the squared error of all of those predictions that we have. This is YI, this is YI hat that we have based on the simple linear regression. What you know from data 8 is that you know that they called this delinear regression that they were teaching you, they called it least the squares regression. We also used exactly the same terminology here. Effectively, simple linear regression pattern or function together with the optimization function that is mean squared error is called least the scores regression because we're trying to find a function that minimizes these scores of the errors that we have over the entire population. So now down to the fun part. I said that if you have the two parts in the model, you have the model kind of Hadron or the template which in this case is simple linear regression and you know what kind of cost function you're using Then everything else is going to be easy fitting the model is going to be easy What I'll do is I'll basically tell you that what does it mean to fit the model when we say that we want to fit the model That means that I want to find a combination of theta 0 and theta 1 values that are giving me the minimum function or the minimum value for the right hand side of this msc function So in order to do so, what you know from your math courses is that I need to calculate the derivative of this function with respect to theta 0, set it to 0 with respect to theta 1, set it equal to 0, find those optimum points, and those optimum points, theta 0 and theta 1 are going to be exactly my theta 0 had on my theta 1 hat that I can use. So the next few kind of or five or so minutes is going to be me driving all of these partial derivatives for you. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

leaf_chunk.audio: So, MSc versus MAE. In the next lecture, I'm going to come back and play with optimizing both of these functions. And I'm going to show you what's the difference between the final model that we'll get depending on which loss function that we use and which empirical response we get at the end. For today's purpose, what I'm going to do is I'm going to say that I'm going to use L2 loss, meaning that I'm going to go ahead and calculate the residual square of each one of those data points in the dataset. And my entire empirical risk function is going to be MSE or mean squared error. Again, this is the mean and this is the squared error of all of those predictions that we have. This is YI, this is YI hat that we have based on the simple linear regression. What you know from data 8 is that you know that they called this delinear regression that they were teaching you, they called it least the squares regression. We also used exactly the same terminology here. Effectively, simple linear regression pattern or function together with the optimization function that is mean squared error is called least the scores regression because we're trying to find a function that minimizes these scores of the errors that we have over the entire population. So now down to the fun part. I said that if you have the two parts in the model, you have the model kind of Hadron or the template which in this case is simple linear regression and you know what kind of cost function you're using Then everything else is going to be easy fitting the model is going to be easy What I'll do is I'll basically tell you that what does it mean to fit the model when we say that we want to fit the model That means that I want to find a combination of theta 0 and theta 1 values that are giving me the minimum function or the minimum value for the right hand side of this msc function So in order to do so, what you know from your math courses is that I need to calculate the derivative of this function with respect to theta 0, set it to 0 with respect to theta 1, set it equal to 0, find those optimum points, and those optimum points, theta 0 and theta 1 are going to be exactly my theta 0 had on my theta 1 hat that I can use. So the next few kind of or five or so minutes is going to be me driving all of these partial derivatives for you.

children.title: Partial Derivatives and MSE Minimization
children.summary: The segment emphasizes the importance of calculus and linear algebra in machine learning, particularly in understanding the process of minimizing Mean Squared Error (MSE) for a Simple Linear Regression (SLR) model. It introduces the concept of taking partial derivatives of the MSE function to find optimal values by setting the derivatives equal to zero, leading to the optimality conditions necessary for regression analysis.

children.title: Calculating MSE Partial Derivatives and Regression Estimators
children.summary: The segment discusses the calculation of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) derivative with respect to model parameters, defining MSE as the average of squared errors and explaining the process of taking its derivative while treating other variables as constants. It emphasizes the importance of partial derivatives with respect to parameters theta zero and theta one for optimizing model parameters, addressing the derivative of summation terms and the application of the chain rule. The derivation of Theta 1 hat in regression is presented, involving manipulation of equations and factoring common terms, leading to a final equation that connects differences between observed and predicted values with the variance of x and correlation. Additionally, it covers solving for unknowns in estimators using definitions of correlation and standard deviation, deriving relationships between variables, and presenting equations that link the correlation coefficient r with standard deviations.

children.title: Calculating Partial Derivatives of MSE
children.summary: The segment covers the calculation of the derivative of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to model parameters. It begins by defining MSE as the average of squared errors, represented by the formula involving residuals (y_i - y_hat_i). The process of taking the derivative of MSE with respect to the parameter theta is explained, treating other variables as constants. The discussion then moves to the partial derivatives of MSE with respect to parameters theta zero and theta one, emphasizing the importance of these derivatives in optimizing model parameters. The derivative of a summation term is also addressed, noting that the derivative of a sum equals the sum of the derivatives, allowing for simplification using the chain rule.

children.title: Calculating the Derivative of MSE
children.summary: The segment discusses the objective function and reiterates the subjective function, which calculates the average of squared errors. The formula involves the residuals (y_i - y_hat_i) squared, representing the mean squared error (MSE). The speaker explains the process of taking the derivative of MSE with respect to the parameter theta, noting that all other variables are treated as constants during this differentiation.

children.title: Derivative of Summation in MSE Calculation
children.summary: The segment explains how to take the derivative of a summation term in the context of Mean Squared Error (MSE). It states that the derivative of a sum equals the sum of the derivatives, allowing the coefficient and summation to be factored out for simplification. The focus is on applying the chain rule to differentiate the function, which is expressed as (yi - θ0 - θ1 * xi)². The derivative with respect to θ0 is calculated, emphasizing that θ0 is treated as a variable while other terms are constants.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: One is that I'm taking the derivative of a summation term, right? And we know that the derivative of sum is equal to sum of the derivatives. So I'm going to take out the coefficient and the summation out of the derivatives so that things are a little bit easier and cleaner. So moving this summation out is is going to give me 1 over n times the summation of pi from 1 up to n of the derivative of what I had, which is yi minus theta zero. minus the one x i. To the power of two, with respect to TETA zero. Right. So first step is easy. I just basically took out the summation and said that the derivative of the summation is equal to some of the derivatives. I'm taking some of those derivatives. to take a derivative with respect to teta 0, teta 0 inside is raised to the power of 2. So you know that in this case, I need to apply chain rule first, meaning that I basically try to take the derivative of the outer function and then take the derivative of the inner function, which in this case is going to be meary writing, the coefficient right here, 1 over n times the summation of i from 1 up to n up. function times the derivative of function. Right? If you think about this as function to the power of two. So what I'll do is I'll say that two times the function which is equal to yi minus is that the zero minus that the one times x i. And then what is the derivative of this function with respect to ? 0 ? So again assuming that everything except for tetasero is a constant term. So the only term that I have to take the derivative is that negative tetasero and derivative of that with respect to tetasero is going to be negative one. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Partial Derivatives of MSE with Respect to Parameters
children.summary: The segment discusses the calculation of the partial derivatives of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to the parameters theta zero and theta one. It simplifies the expression by taking out constants and focuses on the summation of the differences between observed values (yi) and the predicted values based on the model parameters. The chain rule is applied in the derivation process, leading to a simplified form of the derivatives.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Right? is easy. I'm just going to clean everything up and just simplifying constants here. This is going to give me negative 2 over n just taking all the constants out, summation of i from 1 up to n of yi minus theta 0 minus theta 1 x i. So we're done with TETA. zero or taking the partial derivative with respect to teta zero. Let's go ahead and do exactly the same thing for teta one. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Partial Derivative of MSE with Respect to Parameters
children.summary: The process of taking the derivative of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to the parameters is discussed. The derivative of a sum is equal to the sum of the derivatives, and the coefficient 1/n is factored out. The focus is on taking the partial derivative with respect to theta 1, treating all other variables as constants. The derivation involves applying the chain rule and simplifying the expression, resulting in terms that include the difference between the observed values and the predicted values.

children.title: Derivative of Mean Squared Error Using Chain Rule
children.summary: The derivative of the function, specifically the Mean Squared Error (MSE), with respect to theta one is derived using the chain rule. The MSE is expressed as one over n times the summation from i equals one to n of the squared difference between y_i and the linear function defined by theta zero and theta one times x_i. The derivative of the green term with respect to theta one is identified as negative x_i, indicating how the MSE changes with respect to changes in theta one.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And it's within a function. So if I want to take the derivative of this function. to the power of two with respect to, um, theta one, this is going to be equal to two times function times the derivative of function with respect to theta one. And that's exactly what I'll do. Uh, and I'm assuming that this is this function f that I'm calling. Um, so I'm going to apply the chain rule exactly. So one over n times the summation of i from one up to n of the function y i minus theta zero minus theta one x i. Now the derivative. So what is the derivative of that green term with respect to theta one? Yes. Negative x i is the derivative, right? So negative x i is my derivative right here. children.title: Residuals and Optimization in MSE
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the conditions for the optimum values of parameters theta zero and theta one in the context of minimizing the Mean Squared Error (MSE). It is explained that the summation of residuals must equal zero at the optimum point, indicating that the residuals cancel each other out. Two equations are established with two unknowns, theta zero and theta one, which are both set to zero for solving. The resulting optimum values are denoted as theta zero hat and theta one hat.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So one is effectively telling me that if I go ahead and set this to be equal to zero, one, the left hand side is effectively telling me that the summation of all of the residuals should be equal to zero. Because that's residual. And that's why they were canceling each other out. That's why they couldn't be a cost function. Right? This is telling me that at the point that theta zero is optimum, the summation of residuals should be equal to zero. And then the right hand side is basically telling me that The summation of residuals times x i's should be equal to zero. Right, we're going to get to that. And what I need to do right now is that I have two equations and I have two unknowns. And whether the unknowns that I have, so I have two equations, there is one unknown, teta zero and there is one unknown, teta one. I said both of them equal to zero and then I solve it for the optimum teta zero and optimum teta one and that will give me teta zero hat and teta one hat, which are the optimum values that we want. children.title: Partial Derivative of MSE and Optimality Conditions
children.summary: The segment discusses the calculation of the partial derivative of Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to parameters 00 and 01. It begins with the MSE formula and applies the chain rule to derive the necessary equations. The optimality conditions are established by setting the derivatives equal to zero, leading to the equations needed to find the best parameter values.

children.title: Estimating Equations and Optimality Conditions
children.summary: The segment discusses estimating equations used to find optimal theta values. It emphasizes that the summation of residuals equals zero and introduces two key equations: one for the average residuals and another related to the mean squared error (MSE). The process involves deriving these equations to simplify them further and set their derivatives to zero to establish optimality conditions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is where we left off. So remember, that was the equation from the left hand side. The second row is the equation from the right hand side. So the summation of residuals is equal to 0. Summation of residuals times xi equal to 0. What I need to do is I need to just go ahead and use these equations that I'm calling them estimating equations because I'm using them in order to estimate optimum theta i values in order to, I'll solve them in order to find out those optimum values exactly. So let's go ahead and do that. So these are the two equations. One over n times the summation of yi minus yi, i hat is equal to zero. And then the second equation is on the right hand side. What I'll do is I'll derive the first step. So deriving the first step means that what does it mean for this equation to be equal to zero? Is there any way that we can simplify it further? children.title: Estimating Equations and Estimators
children.summary: The segment discusses the formulation of estimating equations to determine parameters θ0 and θ1. It outlines the process of expressing the equations, specifically focusing on the summation of terms related to the observed values (yi) and the predicted values (θ0 + θ1xi). The goal is to solve these equations, which include conditions such as the average of the differences between observed and predicted values being equal to zero.

children.title: Estimating Equations and Estimators Overview
children.summary: The goal is to choose parameters 00 and 01 to solve two estimating equations. The first equation is the sum of the residuals, Tyi - yi = 0, and the second involves the product of the residuals and the independent variable, (yi - Vi).xi = 0. This leads to the formulation of an estimator based on the average of the residuals, represented as (yi - Ô0 - 01xi) = 0.

children.title: Optimal Values and Estimating Equations
children.summary: The segment discusses deriving optimal values for parameters theta 0 and theta 1 by calculating their partial derivatives and setting them equal to zero. This results in theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat, representing the optimum values. The goal is to solve two estimating equations to find the best values, which involves setting the mean squared error (MSE) derivatives to zero.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Because it's optimized to zero. Remember in the previous slide when I was on the previous slide here. Actually, yeah, right here when I was driving all of these values, the tetah 0 and tetah 1, they were just normal tetah 0 and tetah 1 of the function. I actually derived it and calculated the partial derivative with respect to tetah 0, with respect to tetah 1. At the point that you actually set it equal to 0, which is what we have right here, I set this equal to 0, then tetah 0 will become tetah 0 hat, because that's the optimum theta 0. And theta 1 will become theta 1 hat, because I'm also setting this one equal to 0. So that's at the point that you're calculating the optimum and you're finding the exact optimum values by setting the partial derivatives equal to 0, that's when theta 0 and theta 1 will turn into theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat. children.title: Optimality Conditions in Estimating Equations
children.summary: To determine the best values in estimating equations, the derivatives are set to zero to derive the optimality conditions. This involves calculating the mean squared error (MSE) and applying it to the equations, specifically focusing on the terms involving the observed values and the model parameters.

children.title: Estimating Equations and Optimality Conditions
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of finding the optimal values for theta zero hat and theta one hat by setting the derivatives of the mean squared error (MSE) equal to zero. It introduces the concept of estimating equations, specifically focusing on two equations that need to be solved: one involving the average of the yi values and the other relating to the residuals of the model. The goal is to determine the values of theta that minimize the error in predictions.

children.title: Derivation of Theta Zero Hat in Regression
children.summary: The segment explains the derivation of the parameter theta zero hat in simple linear regression. It starts by summing a constant value, theta zero hat, n times, leading to the conclusion that the average value is still theta zero hat. The last term involves pulling out theta one hat from the summation, resulting in theta one hat multiplied by the average of x values (x bar). This leads to the equation y bar minus theta zero hat minus theta one hat times x bar equals zero. The segment connects this derivation to the previous understanding that theta zero hat equals y bar minus the slope times x bar, reinforcing the relationship between the parameters in the context of the cost function defined by mean squared error.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Teta 0 hat is a constant value. I'm summing it up n times, that this is going to be n times Teta 0 hat, and then I'm multiplying 1 over n. So this is equal to just teta zero hat. So the second term is teta zero hat. And then what is the last term? So the last term, there's no easy way to simplify the except for saying that I'm going to pull out theta one hat outside of the summation because it's a constant value. So then I'll have theta one hat times the summation of i from one up to n of x i's, which this one right here is also going to be x bar because that's the Sorry, there's one over n. One over n times the summation of x i's is going to be x bar. Again, setting it equal to zero, kind of cleaning up what I wrote here. y bar minus teta zero, hat minus. PEPTA 1 Times X bar is going to be equal to zero. Right? What does this tell you? This one is telling you that remember when I was calculating the y interest I'm always saying that teta zero hat is equal to y bar minus a slope times x bar. This is exactly that. This is deriving exactly the same equation for the teta zero hat that we had from before. just drive the true. The crazy sequence of defining the simple linear regression to going through the finding the cost function we chose mean a squared error as our cost function. We calculated partial derivatives, we set partial derivatives to equal to zero in order to find out what is the optimum tetaheus zero valiant that's a one value. And then it just happens that if I simplify the first function or the first derivation, it's going to tell me that your theta 0 hat is going to be equal to y bar minus the slope times x bar, which is what we know from a slight tree of today's lecture. But now we systematically drive that and now we have a formal way of defining the objective function. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Estimating Equations and Estimators
children.summary: The focus is on determining the parameters theta hat (θ₀, θ₁) by solving two estimating equations. The equations are presented as: 1) L(yi - θ₀ - θ₁xi) = 0, and 2) ∑(yi - θ₀ - θ₁xi) = 0. The derivation process is outlined but not fully executed manually. The goal is to isolate θ₀ and θ₁ from the combined equations to find their estimates.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The only thing that we need to now do is to figure out what is this theta 1 hat. But we still have two equations. We just looked at one of the equations. If we combine the two equations together, which I'm not going to try to derive it manually, but I'm going to walk you through the steps, is... Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Deriving Theta 1 Hat and Estimators in Regression
children.summary: The segment covers the derivation of the formula for Theta 1 hat in regression by manipulating equation 2 and incorporating the average of x (x bar). It involves factoring out the common term (y i - y i hat) and substituting y i hat with its equation, leading to a final equation that relates differences between y i and y bar, x i and x bar, and incorporates the variance of x, connecting it to correlation. Additionally, the discussion addresses solving for unknowns in estimators by applying definitions of correlation and standard deviation, deriving the relationship between variables, and presenting equations that include the correlation formula and its components to calculate the unknown parameter Ô1 in relation to the correlation coefficient r and standard deviations.

children.title: Deriving Theta 1 Hat in Regression
children.summary: The segment focuses on deriving the formula for Theta 1 hat in a regression context. It begins with the manipulation of equation 2, incorporating the average of x (x bar) to address the x i term. The speaker factors out the common term (y i - y i hat) and substitutes y i hat with its equation, which includes Theta 0 and Theta 1. The derivation leads to an equation where all variables are known except for Theta 1 hat, allowing for further simplification. The final equation relates the differences between y i and y bar, x i and x bar, and incorporates the variance of x, linking it to the concept of correlation.

The equations presented include the correlation formula and its components, emphasizing the calculation of the unknown parameter Ô1 in relation to the correlation coefficient r and standard deviations.

children.title: Objective Functions and Model Evaluation Techniques
children.summary: Machine learning modeling requires defining a mathematical objective function, with examples like simple linear regression demonstrating how different parameter values affect outcomes. The effectiveness of these functions is assessed using a cost function, specifically mean squared error (MSE), and optimal parameter values are determined through partial derivatives. Key points include that the average and sum of residuals equal zero, indicating orthogonality to predicted variables. Model evaluation techniques include visualizing data, calculating MSE and root mean squared error (RMSE), and visualizing residuals to check regression line suitability. The next class will introduce constant models and various loss functions, emphasizing the importance of visualizing data and using performance metrics like RMSE.

children.title: Defining Objective Functions in Machine Learning
children.summary: Machine learning modeling involves defining a mathematical objective function rather than guessing. The model pattern is established, such as in simple linear regression, where different values for parameters (theta 0 and theta 1) yield different functions. The effectiveness of these functions is evaluated using a cost function, specifically the mean squared error, and partial derivatives are calculated to determine optimal parameter values (theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat). Key notes include that the average of residuals equals zero and the sum of residuals also equals zero, indicating that residuals are orthogonal to the predicted variables. This concept will be explored further in the next session.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This kind of sounds like magic, but now we formally define how actual machine learning people do modeling. They don't guess. They basically have a mathematical objective function that they define. They define the family of functions or the model pattern, which is what we did, but define what is the equation for simple linear regression. But we said that, okay, depending on the tetasero and tetabone value, it's going to be a different function or a different line that we're mapping. How do we find out how good that function is with defined the cost function? We actually use minus squared error, we actually venture calculating partial derivatives in order to find out, Find out the optimum values for teta 0 and teta 1, which got us the teta 0 hat and teta 1 hat that we have. So this is where we are. What we got at the end was that was the two kind of final notes that I told you when I was doing the derivation. I said that the average of residuals is equal to zero or the summation of residuals is equal to zero. And I also said that the residual multiplied with the x i is equal to zero. I think on Tuesday of next week, we're going to talk about the fact that this means that the residuals are orthogonal to the predicted variables. And we're going to see what that means. This is two vectors are orthogonal to each other and that's how the shape of the line is always is the terministically calculated once you have all of the data points that you have. children.title: Model Evaluation Techniques in Data Analysis
children.summary: Evaluating model performance involves three key methods: visualizing data and computing statistics, calculating mean squared error (MSE) and its square root (root mean squared error, RMSE), and visualizing residuals. Visualization helps assess the correlation coefficient, which informs whether linear regression analysis is appropriate. MSE quantifies model error, and RMSE converts this error back to the original data units for meaningful interpretation. Residual visualization checks the suitability of the regression line across different datasets, ensuring residuals do not exhibit patterns that indicate model inadequacy.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We have two more minutes, but I just want to throw it out because you need for you need this for discussions that are happening today and that's about evaluating the model. Your TAs are going to talk about evaluating the model a little bit in discussions today, but effectively that's the last step of the data of the modeling that we're going to go through. There are three things that we're doing generally even we're going to evaluate how well the model is performing. performing. The first thing is that a lot of time we would go ahead and visualize the data and we will compute the statistics. Again, we will calculate the correlation coefficient if the correlation coefficient is acceptable then we do the linear regression analysis. The other way that we evaluate the performance of the model is that we will calculate mean a squared error and we will calculate the square root of that. That's called root minus the square root error. And the reason that we calculate the square root of it is because we want to turn the error into the original unit of the data. Because the error is in the second order. It's when we're calculating a square root of the errors, we were squaring the error. So the unit was in the squared units. So by taking a square root, we're going back to the original unit of the data. So that value is going to be meaningful if you're doing any estimation. The last thing that we will do, and this is what you will see in discussions, is we will visualize your residuals. So when we go ahead and visualize residuals, what we want to see is that if these are the four different data sets that I'm dealing with and I'm passing the regression line through them, you can kind of see that the regression line matches one or two of them perfectly, but it's not a suitable choice for the rest of them. If you go ahead and visualize the residual, meaning that on the y-axis, you plot the residuals and the x-axis, you plot x, we don't want this residual plot to have any kind of pattern. Which is what you're seeing in this first case that residual plot does not show any pattern, but you're seeing that residual plot absolutely shows the pattern, meaning that linear regression was not a good choice. Or residual plot, in this case also shows the pattern. also in this case shows the pattern, right? So what we want at the end is that once you build the residual plot, you really want to make sure that the errors are kind of randomly distributed and equally distributed across X and also across the residual kind of dimension. leaf_chunk.audio: We have two more minutes, but I just want to throw it out because you need for you need this for discussions that are happening today and that's about evaluating the model. Your TAs are going to talk about evaluating the model a little bit in discussions today, but effectively that's the last step of the data of the modeling that we're going to go through. There are three things that we're doing generally even we're going to evaluate how well the model is performing. performing. The first thing is that a lot of time we would go ahead and visualize the data and we will compute the statistics. Again, we will calculate the correlation coefficient if the correlation coefficient is acceptable then we do the linear regression analysis. The other way that we evaluate the performance of the model is that we will calculate mean a squared error and we will calculate the square root of that. That's called root minus the square root error. And the reason that we calculate the square root of it is because we want to turn the error into the original unit of the data. Because the error is in the second order. It's when we're calculating a square root of the errors, we were squaring the error. So the unit was in the squared units. So by taking a square root, we're going back to the original unit of the data. So that value is going to be meaningful if you're doing any estimation. The last thing that we will do, and this is what you will see in discussions, is we will visualize your residuals. So when we go ahead and visualize residuals, what we want to see is that if these are the four different data sets that I'm dealing with and I'm passing the regression line through them, you can kind of see that the regression line matches one or two of them perfectly, but it's not a suitable choice for the rest of them. If you go ahead and visualize the residual, meaning that on the y-axis, you plot the residuals and the x-axis, you plot x, we don't want this residual plot to have any kind of pattern. Which is what you're seeing in this first case that residual plot does not show any pattern, but you're seeing that residual plot absolutely shows the pattern, meaning that linear regression was not a good choice. Or residual plot, in this case also shows the pattern. also in this case shows the pattern, right? So what we want at the end is that once you build the residual plot, you really want to make sure that the errors are kind of randomly distributed and equally distributed across X and also across the residual kind of dimension.

children.title: Introduction to Constant Model and Loss Functions
children.summary: The discussion will cover constant models and various loss functions in the next class. Ideal model evaluation steps include visualizing original data, computing statistics, and using performance metrics like RMSE for simple linear least squares models. Additionally, residual visualization is emphasized, noting that a good regression plot should show no discernible pattern in the residuals.
